elements.sent-2411-001: Smart inverters are instrumental in the integration of renewable and distributed energy resources (DERs) into the electric grid.
elements.sent-2411-002: Such inverters rely on communication layers for continuous control and monitoring, potentially exposing them to cyber-physical attacks such as false data injection attacks (FDIAs).
elements.sent-2411-003: We propose to construct a defense strategy against a priori unknown FDIAs with a multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) framework.
elements.sent-2411-004: The first agent is an adversary that simulates and discovers various FDIA strategies, while the second agent is a defender in charge of detecting and localizing FDIAs.
elements.sent-2411-005: This approach enables the defender to be trained against new FDIAs continuously generated by the adversary.
elements.sent-2411-006: The numerical results demonstrate that the proposed MARL defender outperforms a supervised offline defender.
elements.sent-2411-007: Additionally, we show that the detection skills of an MARL defender can be combined with that of an offline defender through a transfer learning approach.
elements.sent-2411-008: Integrating distributed energy resources (DERs) into an electric grid inherently reduces the grid's inertia, making it less resilient to frequency instabilities \cite{denholm2020inertia .}
elements.sent-2411-009: Smart inverters are a promising solution that can help regulate voltage and frequency, thanks to actuation strategies that depend on the state of the system \cite{tamrakar2017vsm_review .}
elements.sent-2411-010: Smart inverters rely on information exchange, either through observations of the state of the grid or through actuation decisions sent via communication networks \cite{Yuanliang .}
elements.sent-2411-011: This makes them a target for cyber-physical attacks, and in particular false data injection attacks (FDIAs) that tamper with sensor measurements \cite{liu2011false or even the control logic of the inverter \cite{nguyen2020electric .}}
elements.sent-2411-012: Cyber-physical attacks are becoming a tangible risk: five consequential attacks were reported in 2023~\cite{DOE , and fifteen FDIAs have been noted over the past ten years~\cite{HABIB2023 .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: To address the risk of FDIAs, research efforts have focused on constructing accurate detection methods.
elements.sent-2411-014: FDIA detection methods typically rely on the comparison of a reference state (without FDIAs) and the actual observed state.
elements.sent-2411-015: This approach can take the form of a state predictor~\cite{sahu2024 or the construction of a state embedding~\cite{Aboelwafa .}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Discrepancies between reference and observed states are then used to decide whether an FDIA has occurred.
elements.sent-2411-017: To realize this detection principle, a data-based approach would involve training a model that distinguishes between FDIA data and benign data.
elements.sent-2411-018: This often requires some level of supervised training where one assumes how FDIAs occur \cite{Ying,Habibi .}
elements.sent-2411-019: If the decision boundary is imperfect, the FDIA detection may become vulnerable to impactful and stealthy adversarial examples.
elements.sent-2411-020: Identifying the vulnerabilities of FDIA detection methods has been the object of several research efforts.
elements.sent-2411-021: Previously, constrained optimization~\cite{Jafari,Choraria and adversarial learning~\cite{Jiwei strategies have been used to defeat bad data detection models.}}
elements.sent-2411-022: The same optimization problem can be formulated with reinforcement learning (RL), which can be used for arbitrarily complex systems while generating hitherto unseen FDIAs by interacting with an environment~\cite{Romesh .}
elements.sent-2411-023: This approach has been used to improve detection methods in the context of frequency control~\cite{Mohamed and state estimation problems~\cite{HUANG2023, Xiaohong .}}
elements.sent-2411-024: These previous works only utilized a single RL agent to serve as either adversary or defender but did not consider concurrent adversarial training.
elements.sent-2411-025: By contrast, we propose a multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) framework for concurrently training an FDIA adversary and defender.
elements.sent-2411-026: One of the key challenges of MARL's training is the non-stationary environment for the competitive agents.
elements.sent-2411-027: To address this, the learning environment and reward function are strategically designed to achieve the simultaneous performance improvement of both the adversary and defender agents.
elements.sent-2411-028: Even if the adversary continuously generates novel FDIAs to disrupt the system frequency, the defender can promptly adjust its behavior and update its defense strategy accordingly.
elements.sent-2411-029: The contribution of this paper is twofold:.
elements.sent-2411-030: Without prior knowledge, we demonstrate how a defender trained with the proposed MARL framework can handle continuously varying unknown adversarial attacks launched by an adversary.
elements.sent-2411-031: We show that prior knowledge from an offline-trained defender can be retained through a transfer learning approach.
elements.sent-2411-032: Experiments show that this strategy enhances the performance of the MARL defender on unseen FDIAs during the MARL training procedure.
elements.sent-2411-033: Sec.~\ref{sec:prob formulates the FDIA detection problem.}
elements.sent-2411-034: Sec.~\ref{sec:method describes the MARL framework and Sec.~\ref{sec:results evaluates its performance.. Finally, Sec.}}
elements.sent-2411-035: Consider a power grid with a set $\mathcal{N}$ of $N$ buses.
elements.sent-2411-036: The system state $\mathbf{s \coloneq [\boldsymbol{\theta ^\top ; \boldsymbol{\omega ^\top]^\top \in \mathbb{R ^{2N}$ contains phase angles and frequency deviation of all buses.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-037: The primary frequency dynamics can be expressed as the swing equation \cite{Wenqi : \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\dot{\theta_i} = \omega_i\, , M_i\dot{\omega_i} = p_{i} - p^ {IBR}_i(\omega_i) - D_i \omega_i - \sum_{j=1}^N B_{ij} \sin{(\theta_i - \theta_j)} \, ,\end{equation} \end{subequations where $M_i$ and $D_i$ represent the inertia and damping coefficients of the $i$-th bus, respectively.}}}
elements.sent-2411-038: The droop coefficients can be designed using conventional optimization methods to stabilize the primary frequency under a random off-equilibrium initial condition \cite{Wenqi .}
elements.sent-2411-039: The power system is time-constrained and each episode consists of $T$ timesteps spanning the time interval $[0, t_f]$.
elements.sent-2411-040: An adversary is assumed to have remote access to all $N$ smart inverters of the system.
elements.sent-2411-041: Its objective is to induce frequency instability by modifying the droop coefficient of each inverter.
elements.sent-2411-042: The adversary can change only one coefficient at a time.
elements.sent-2411-043: In the absence of detection, the adversary aims to solve the following optimization problem \cite{Romesh : \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\max_{k_{i, t}^\prime} \, \, \sum_{t \in T} \sum_{i \in N}|\omega_{i, t}| - |\omega_{i, t}^{ref}| \, , \label{eq:ob1} s.t. \quad Eq. \, \eqref{eq:swing} \, , \label{eq:swing1} p^{IBR}_{i,t} = k_{i, t}^\prime w_{i, t} \, , \label{eq:ibr} \|k^ref - k_{t}^\prime \|_0 \leq 1 \, , \label{eq:change}\end{equation} \end{subequations where $\omega_i^{\text{ref }$ denotes the frequency deviation of bus $i$ using the unaltered droop controller.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}\max_{k_{i, t}^\prime} \, \, \sum_{t \in T} \sum_{i \in N}|\omega_{i, t}| - |\omega_{i, t}^{ref}| \, , \label{eq:ob1} s.t. \quad Eq. \, \eqref{eq:swing} \, , \label{eq:swing1} p^{IBR}_{i,t} = k_{i, t}^\prime w_{i, t} \, , \label{eq:ibr} \|k^ref - k_{t}^\prime \|_0 \leq 1 \, , \label{eq:change}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-044: The objective function \eqref{eq:ob1 indicates the adversary aims to tamper with the droop coefficient (denoted by $k_i^\prime$) to maximize frequency deviation in the control horizon $\mathcal{T \coloneq \{0, \cdots T-1\}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-045: Eq.~\eqref{eq:change ensures that at most one droop coefficient can be modified by the adversary at any timestep, where $\mathbf{k '_t \in \mathbb{R ^N$ denotes the vector of (tampered) droop coefficients at time $t$ and $\mathbf{k ^\text{ref}$ are the unaltered droop coefficients of all buses.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-046: The detection method considered uses a long short-term memory (LSTM) state predictor that was proposed in Ref.~\cite{sahu2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-047: The LSTM predicts the reference state at time $t$ based on the observed states over the past $d-1$ timesteps, i.e., $[\mathbf{s _{t-(d - 1) , \cdots \mathbf{s _{t - 1 ] \mapsto \mathbf{\hat{s _t$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-048: The defender is a multiclass classifier that localizes the attacked bus index based on the state prediction error, i.e., $\mathbf{s ^e_t \mapsto g^d_t \in \{-1, 0, \cdots, N-1 \}$, where $\mathbf{s _t^\mathrm{e}\coloneq c_{w (\mathbf{s _t - \mathbf{\hat{s _t)$, where $c_{w}$ is a scaling coefficient and the class $-1$ implies that no attack occurs.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-049: The architecture of the offline defender is schematically shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:offline_dec .}
elements.sent-2411-050: Although the detection is called periodically every $d$ timesteps, it can detect FDIAs that occurred over the past $d-1$ timesteps~\cite{sahu2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-051: The ensemble of detection timesteps is denoted by $\mathcal{T _d$ and $\operatorname{card (\mathcal{T _d) = T/d$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-052: To train the supervised classifier, one first synthetically constructs FDIAs, as described hereafter.
elements.sent-2411-053: At timestep $t$, if an FDIA occurs, the adversary modifies the droop coefficient of bus $i$.
elements.sent-2411-054: Otherwise, the unaltered droop coefficients are used.
elements.sent-2411-055: Throughout the episode, the training data is constructed by randomly selecting timesteps where an FDIA occurs.
elements.sent-2411-056: Let $T_a$ denote the fraction of episode steps where an FDIA occurs and we have $T_a \in \{0.16, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8 \}$ to simulate different adversarial scenarios (e.g., $T_a = 0.8$ implies an FDIA occurs for $80\%$ of the episode).
elements.sent-2411-057: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:data_gen , the dataset comprises windows of length $d$.}
elements.sent-2411-058: At most one bus is attacked per window, thereby alleviating any ambiguity about how each window is labeled.
elements.sent-2411-059: The offline defender will serve as a baseline for comparison with the MARL defender in Sec.~\ref{sec:results .}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.7\linewidth]{data_gen.png} \caption{The demonstration of defender's label under FDIAs at bus $i$. $g^d$ denotes the attacked bus index identified by the defender (or -1 if no attack).} \label{fig:data_gen}
elements.sent-2411-060: Figure~\ref{fig:overall_architecture illustrates the proposed MARL framework for proactive FDIA detection.}
elements.sent-2411-061: The defender agent is a classifier that aims at detecting the bus under attack.
elements.sent-2411-062: The adversary agent seeks to disrupt the power grid frequency while avoiding being captured by the defender.
elements.sent-2411-063: The adversary's observation space, $\mathbf{s _t^a$, is the concatenation of the system state and the normalized simulation step, i.e. $\mathbf{s _t^a \coloneq[\mathbf{\boldsymbol{\theta ^\top, \boldsymbol{\omega ^\top, t/T]^\top$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-064: The normalized simulation step provides the current time to the adversary.
elements.sent-2411-065: The adversary's action space is $\mathbf{a _t = [g_t^A, c_t, m_t]$, where $g_t^A \in \{-1, 0, \cdots, N-1 \}$ represents the index of the bus attacked or $-1$ for no attack, $c_t$ is value with which the droop coefficient is replaced ($k_{i, t ^\prime = c_t$), and $m_t$ is a boolean used to mute or not the attack.}}
elements.sent-2411-066: For simplification and consistency with refs.~\cite{sahu2024,Romesh , $c_t \in \{-1, \, 0, \, 1\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-067: Given the detection time $t \in \mathcal{T _d$, the detection window is defined as $\mathcal{D _{t}\coloneq [t-(d-1), \cdots, t]$.}}
elements.sent-2411-068: We define $g^a$ to record the attack bus index over $\mathcal{D _{t}$ and initialize it as $-1$.}
elements.sent-2411-069: To mimic the synthetically generated FDIAs used for offline training, the following post-processing action mask mechanism is used to ensure that at most one bus is under attack over the detection window $\mathcal{D _{t}$: \begin{equation}g^a_t =  g^A_t \, , if \, g^a = -1\, , g^a\, , if \, m_t = 0 \land g^a \neq -1\, , -1 \, , if \, m_t = 1 \land g^a \neq -1,  \label{action_pp}\end{equation} where, $\land$ denotes the logical AND operator.}
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}g^a_t =  g^A_t \, , if \, g^a = -1\, , g^a\, , if \, m_t = 0 \land g^a \neq -1\, , -1 \, , if \, m_t = 1 \land g^a \neq -1,  \label{action_pp}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-070: Once the bus to attack is chosen with $g_t^A$, the binary variable $m_t$ is used to decide whether to attack ($m_t = 0$) or mute the attack ($m_t = 1$).
elements.sent-2411-071: The stealthiness constraint is included in the new objective function Eq.~\eqref{eq:problem_dec through a penalty $p$ for being detected ($p < 0$).}
elements.sent-2411-072: A large reward value reflects that the adversary induced frequency instability, and that it escaped the defender.
elements.sent-2411-073: Hereafter, the adversary is referred to as MARL-A.
elements.sent-2411-074: The defender agent is the multiclass classifier described in Sec.~\ref{sec:LSTM-based .}
elements.sent-2411-075: The LSTM is held frozen during the MARL training process.
elements.sent-2411-077: The action space $g_t^d \in \{-1, 0, \cdots, N-1 \}$ represents the identified attacked bus index or $-1$ for no attack.
elements.sent-2411-078: If the defender successfully identifies the attack status, it will receive a reward $r > 0$ and a penalty $-r$ otherwise.
elements.sent-2411-079: The instantaneous reward at time $t$ is defined in Eq.~\ref{eq:reward_d .}
elements.sent-2411-080: The defender aims to maximize the cumulative reward $\max_{g_t^d \, \, \sum_{t \in \mathcal{T r_{t ^{d}$. \begin{equation}r_t^d =  r \, , g_t^a = g_t^d \,, t \in T_d \, , -r \, , g_t^a \neq g_t^d \, , t \in T_d \,, 0 \, , t \in T \setminus T_d \, .  \label{eq:reward_d}\end{equation} In the rest of the text, the defender is referred to as MARL-D.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation}r_t^d =  r \, , g_t^a = g_t^d \,, t \in T_d \, , -r \, , g_t^a \neq g_t^d \, , t \in T_d \,, 0 \, , t \in T \setminus T_d \, .  \label{eq:reward_d}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-081: The concurrent training procedure of the attacker and defender is described in Algorithm \ref{alg:marl .}
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Multi-agent framework for FDIA detection.}\label{alg:marl} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \For{episode $e = 1,2, \cdots K$} \; \State Reset the initial state $\mathbf{s}_0$. \For {simulation step $t = 0, 1, \cdots, T - 1$} \State Obtain the attack status $g^a_t$ based on Eq.~\eqref{action_pp} and $c_t$ from the adversary's policy network $\mathcal{A}_{\alpha}(\mathbf{a_t}|\mathbf{s_t})$. \State Calculate $\mathbf{s}_{t+1}$ using the swing equation and obtain the reward $r_t^a = r^\omega_t$. \If {$t \in \mathcal{T}_d$} \label{line:defender_start} \State Apply the defender's policy network $\mathcal{D}_{\theta}({g_t^d}|\mathbf{s_t})$ and obtain the detected bus index $g^d_t$. \If {$g^d_t = g_t^a $ } \label{line:observation} \State The reward of the defender is $r^d_t = r$. \Else \State The penalty to the defender is $r^d_t = -r$. \EndIf \label{line:defender_end} \If{$g^d_t = g_t^a \land g_t^a \neq -1 $} \label{line:attacker_reward_start} \State The penalty to the adversary is $r^a_t = p$ \EndIf \State Calculate the total reward of the adversary over the detection window $R_t = \sum_{t \in \mathcal{D}_t}r_{t}^a$. \label{line:attacker_reward_end} \EndIf \EndFor \State Update the parameters of the policy networks. \EndFor \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-082: Ideally, the adversarial training of the defender refines an offline-trained one instead of training it from scratch.
elements.sent-2411-083: This way, expert knowledge can be distilled into the defender, and it can still be made robust to adversarial examples.
elements.sent-2411-084: To achieve this goal, we use the offline defender described in Sec.~\ref{sec:LSTM-based to initialize the policy neural network of the MARL-D.}
elements.sent-2411-085: The goal of this procedure is to improve the defense strategy of the MARL-D against new adversarial attacks, while retaining the prior knowledge of the offline-trained defender.
elements.sent-2411-086: In the remainder of the text, MARL-D refers to an agent trained from a random weight initialization, while a TF-MARL-D is a defender for which transfer learning is used, and TF-MARL-A denotes its corresponding adversary.
elements.sent-2411-087: Note that transfer learning is only applied to the defender; MARL-A and TF-MARL-A always use a random weight initialization.
elements.sent-2411-088: The proposed framework is tested on the 10-bus Kron reduced IEEE New England 39-bus system, for which unaltered droop coefficients are provided in \cite{Wenqi .}
elements.sent-2411-089: Here, $t_f$ = 5s, and the simulation interval is $0.01\rm{s}$ leading to a total number of steps per episode $T=500$.
elements.sent-2411-090: Consistently with Ref.~\cite{sahu2024 , the window length is set to $d = 6$ and the ensemble of detection timesteps is therefore $\mathcal{T _d : = \{ 6, 12, \cdots, 498\} $.}}
elements.sent-2411-091: Initial conditions are constructed by superimposing disturbances onto the equilibrium state.
elements.sent-2411-092: Disturbances are sampled from $\mathcal{U (-0.2, 0.2)$ for both the phase and frequency.}
elements.sent-2411-093: The same initial condition is used in MARL and offline defender training.
elements.sent-2411-094: In the adversary reward (Eq.~\ref{eq:r_a_all ), $c_s = 0.1$ to avoid large training reward values and stabilize the training process.}
elements.sent-2411-095: The reward for a successful defense and the penalty for being captured are given as $r = -p = 0.1$, and $c_{w}$ is set to $100$.
elements.sent-2411-096: We use the Ray library with TensorFlow to train MARL using the proximal policy optimization (PPO) algorithm \cite{moritz2018ray , where the entropy coefficient and the clip range are set to $0.01$ and $0.2$, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-097: The LSTM is the same as the one described in Ref.~\cite{sahu2024 and uses 100 units.}
elements.sent-2411-098: The policy neural networks of the adversary and defender contain two hidden layers, each consisting of $256$ neurons with a hyperbolic tangent ($\tanh$) activation function.
elements.sent-2411-099: The train-batch and mini-batch sizes are set to $10^5$ and $128$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-100: The learning rate is set to $10^{-4}$, and the rollout fragment length is set to $500$.
elements.sent-2411-101: We implement the simulations using the high performance computing (HPC) system in parallel across multiple nodes with multiple CPU cores.
elements.sent-2411-102: Figure~\ref{fig:train illustrates the training rewards under $5$ independent simulation runs.}
elements.sent-2411-103: In the following figures, only the results of the training run that leads to the highest accuracy for the MARL-D are shown.
elements.sent-2411-104: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:coe , the adversary typically chooses $c_t=-1$ because a negative value of $c_t$ leads to more impactful attacks \cite{Romesh .}}
elements.sent-2411-105: Figure~\ref{fig:fre_train shows the resulting frequency instability of each bus caused by the MARL-A.}
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=0.7\linewidth]{train.pdf} \caption{History of the agent's rewards as a function of epoch. The solid line and the shaded area are the mean and standard deviation of the reward obtained with five training runs (faded color curves).} \label{fig:train}
elements.figure-2411-005: \subfloat[Droop coefficient $c_t$ value modified by MARL-A. \label{fig:coe}]{ \includegraphics[width=0.48\linewidth]{coeff.pdf}} \subfloat[Frequency deviation of each bus. \label{fig:fre_train}]{ \includegraphics[width=0.5\linewidth]{fre.pdf}} \caption{Action of MARL-A over one episode and its physical effect.} \label{control_123_25p}
elements.sent-2411-106: As shown in Table \ref{tab:p_com_nowup , the MARL-D outperforms the offline defender against adversarial attacks in all the independent simulation runs.}
elements.sent-2411-107: Despite different adversaries, the MARL-D achieves more than $60\%$ detection accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-108: In contrast, for the third training run, the offline defender accuracy dropped to $36\%$ for the MARL-A attack.
elements.sent-2411-109: This is a remarkable result since MARL-A does not have knowledge of the synthetic FDIA used to train the offline defender, and suggests that data-based FDIA defenders are vulnerable to examples that deviate from the training dataset.
elements.sent-2411-110: Figure~\ref{fig:det_action shows the attacked bus index and the identified attacked bus by the MARL-D and the offline defender.}
elements.sent-2411-111: Both the offline defender and the MARL-D successfully catch the attacked bus index $6$, which indicates that continuously attacking the same bus is likely to be captured.
elements.sent-2411-112: However, the MARL-D outperforms the offline defender when the adversary rapidly varies its action.
elements.figure-2411-006: \begin{center} \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.15} \caption{Detection accuracy (\%) under MARL-A's generated attacks of multiple independent training runs} \label{tab:p_com_nowup} \begin{tabular}{ |c|c|c|c|c|c| } $\sum_{t \in \mathcal{T}}r_t^w$ $44$ $52$ $34$ $52$ $34$ MARL-D $63\%$ $65\%$ $71\%$ $71\%$ $77\%$ Offline defender $58\%$ $60\%$ $36\%$ $61\%$ $43\%$ \end{tabular} \end{center}
elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth]{det_action.pdf} \caption{Attacked bus by MARL-A and the detected bus by MARL-D. A successful defense implies $g_t^a = g_t^d $ and the detection accuracy is $77 \%$. } \label{fig:det_action}
elements.figure-2411-008: \includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth]{offline_det_action.pdf} \caption{The attacked bus by MARL-A and the detected bus by the offline defender. A successful defense implies $g_t^a = g_t^d$, and the detection accuracy is $43\%$. } \label{fig:offline_det_action}
elements.sent-2411-113: In this section, we investigate whether it is reasonable to rely on an MARL training framework to construct a detection method that generalizes well to unseen attacks.
elements.sent-2411-114: These refer to FDIAs that may not be discovered by the MARL-A during the training process.
elements.sent-2411-115: For this experiment, we gauge our defender's performance against FDIAs that would cause the most system disruption, i.e., when the adversary chooses $c_t = -1$ over the entire control horizon (which is referred to as time-invariant attack) \cite{Romesh .}
elements.sent-2411-116: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:p_com_invi , the offline defender outperforms the MARL-D except for bus $6$, which is often targeted by the MARL-A (Fig.~\ref{fig:det_action ).}}
elements.sent-2411-117: This shows that although MARL-A can discover a diverse set of attacks that help improve the detection performance of MARL-D, it cannot be expected to have exhaustively explored the space of FDIAs.
elements.sent-2411-118: For that reason, despite being robust to adversarial examples, MARL-D might not generalize well to FDIAs that were synthetically generated in the offline defender's training dataset.
elements.sent-2411-119: A preferable solution is to combine the benefit of the expert knowledge available to the offline defender with the adversarial robustness of MARL-D.
elements.sent-2411-120: Here knowledge transfer can help achieve this goal.
elements.sent-2411-121: A TF-MARL-D defender is therefore trained to this end.
elements.sent-2411-122: The performances of the TF-MARL-D are on par or even outperform (buses $2$, $4$, and $8$) the offline defender for time-invariant attacks, which shows that the transfer learning strategy is viable to retain prior knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-123: Table \ref{tab:p_com_wup shows that although the performance of TF-MARL-D is enhanced for time-invariant attacks, it still consistently outperforms the offline defender when exposed to the TF-MARL-A adversary (performance increases between 40\% and 225\% across $5$ independent runs).}
elements.sent-2411-124: Compared to MARL-A (Table \ref{tab:p_com_nowup first row), the total frequency deviation induced by TF-MARL-A is reduced MARL-A.}
elements.sent-2411-125: This indicates that the warm-up strategy encouraged the adversary to explore less impactful and more stealthy FDIA.
elements.figure-2411-010: \begin{center} \renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.15} \caption{Detection accuracy (\%) under TF-MARL-A's generated attacks of multiple independent training runs.} \label{tab:p_com_wup} \begin{tabular}{ |c|c|c|c|c|c| } $\sum_{t \in \mathcal{T}}r_t^w$ 20 19 21 22 18 TF-MARL-D $33\%$ $39\%$ $39\%$ $42\%$ $46\%$ Offline defender $22\%$ $20\%$ $12\%$ $19\%$ $33\%$ \end{tabular} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-126: We described and demonstrated an MARL framework that can help craft a defense strategy against stealthy and impactful FDIAs.
elements.sent-2411-127: Compared to an offline defender, the MARL defender can continuously learn to defend against newly generated adversarial FDIAs and adjust its defense strategy promptly.
elements.sent-2411-128: However, we show that MARL is not sufficient to ensure that a defender can catch all possible FDIAs.
elements.sent-2411-129: Instead, it is preferable to combine, through transfer learning, the expert knowledge from an offline defender with the MARL defender to enhance its robustness against adversarial examples.. \bibliographystyle{IEEEtran.}
